paragraphs.text: Driven by advances in vision-language models (VLMs), mobile GUI agents that autonomously interact with smartphone interfaces have attracted considerable research interest~\cite{aitw,cogagent,appagent,auto-ui}. The \textsc{Android in the Wild} (AITW) dataset~\cite{aitw} provided a large-scale benchmark with diverse real-world tasks and gesture-based interactions, establishing a foundation for training and evaluating device-control systems. CogAgent~\cite{cogagent} demonstrated strong visual understanding of GUI screenshots through a specialized visual encoder and achieved state-of-the-art results on multiple GUI benchmarks. AppAgent~\cite{appagent} leveraged multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to operate smartphone applications through a flexible exploration-based framework. Auto-UI~\cite{auto-ui} introduced a chain-of-action mechanism that fuses historical action sequences with future action plans to enable screen-level interaction without relying on environment parsing.

More recent work has focused on open-source VLM-based GUI agents that approach the capabilities of commercial models. SeeClick~\cite{seeclick} proposed GUI grounding pre-training to enhance LVLMs with explicit instruction-to-element localization, demonstrating that grounding accuracy directly correlates with downstream task success. UGround~\cite{uground} introduced a universal visual grounding framework operating purely on pixel-level perception, constructing a large-scale grounding dataset and achieving strong results without text-based representations. OS-ATLAS~\cite{osatlas} developed a foundation action model for cross-platform GUI grounding, leveraging a large-scale multi-platform corpus and unified action labeling to achieve strong out-of-distribution generalization. UI-TARS~\cite{uitars} pioneered a native GUI agent model that perceives screenshots directly, incorporating System-2 reasoning for multi-step planning and iterative online training for continuous self-improvement. Falcon-UI~\cite{falconui} proposed a decoupled training paradigm that first learns GUI context understanding from raw interaction sequences before instruction-following, demonstrating that deep GUI comprehension enables effective downstream task execution.

Several datasets and benchmarks have been proposed to facilitate mobile GUI agent research. AMEX~\cite{amex} introduced multi-level annotations including interactive element grounding, functional descriptions, and stepwise action chains to support more robust agent training. AndroidControl~\cite{androidcontrol} provided a large-scale dataset with both high-level and low-level instructions, enabling systematic study of in-domain and out-of-domain generalization for UI control agents.

Reasoning plays a critical role in GUI agents, particularly for tasks requiring multi-step planning and context-aware decision-making. Android in the Zoo~\cite{aitz} introduced the Chain-of-Action-Thought (CoaT) paradigm, which explicitly models semantic reasoning in GUI navigation by capturing screen context, action thinking, target specification, and outcome prediction. The CoaT pattern has been shown to significantly improve performance on complex actions such as clicking and typing, particularly when combined with fine-tuning on the AITZ benchmark. The thinking--decision--grounding triplet underlying CoaT has been widely validated as an effective reasoning structure in subsequent GUI works.

However, the effectiveness of chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning for GUI agents remains nuanced. Li et al.~\cite{cot-mobile} conducted an empirical study revealing that CoT reasoning yields inconsistent performance gains in mobile GUI agent tasks, with improvements observed in interactive environments but minimal or even negative effects on static benchmarks. Their findings suggest that reasoning benefits depend heavily on model architecture, task characteristics, and benchmark design. UI-TARS~\cite{uitars} incorporated System-2 reasoning through explicit task decomposition, reflection, and milestone recognition, demonstrating that structured multi-step planning can benefit GUI agents when integrated into the training pipeline.

Self-training has emerged as a key paradigm for improving language model reasoning capabilities. Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)~\cite{dpo} introduced a method for aligning language models with preferences without training a separate reward model, enabling efficient fine-tuning through pairwise comparison of preferred and dispreferred responses. Building on DPO, several works have explored preference optimization at finer granularities. Step-DPO~\cite{stepdpo} proposed treating individual reasoning steps as units for preference learning, achieving significant accuracy improvements on mathematical reasoning tasks. Meanwhile, DeepSeek-R1~\cite{deepseekr1} demonstrated that large-scale reinforcement learning with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) can incentivize strong reasoning capabilities in LLMs without supervised fine-tuning as a prerequisite.

In the GUI domain, self-training approaches have recently been adapted to improve agent reasoning. GUI-G1~\cite{guig1} analyzed R1-Zero-like training for GUI grounding, identifying critical challenges including excessive reasoning degrading grounding performance due to image token overload, reward hacking in hit-based reward functions, and length biases in GRPO optimization. Their proposed Fast Thinking Template and box-size-constrained reward design achieved strong results with limited training data.
\subsection{Monte Carlo Tree Search for LLM Reasoning}

Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) has been extensively adapted from game-playing domains to enhance the reasoning capabilities of large language models. Tree of Thoughts~\cite{tot} introduced a deliberate problem-solving framework that enables language models to explore multiple reasoning paths through tree-structured search, demonstrating significant improvements on tasks requiring planning and lookahead. Building on this paradigm, Xie et al.~\cite{mcts-dpo} proposed an iterative preference learning framework that uses MCTS to generate step-level preference signals from LLM-generated responses, enabling fine-grained policy updates via DPO. Their work demonstrated that breaking instance-level rewards into granular step-level feedback yields superior reasoning performance, and that on-policy data collection is essential for stable self-improvement.

ReST-MCTS*~\cite{restmcts} developed a reinforcement learning framework that uses MCTS with process reward guidance to generate high-quality reasoning traces without manual per-step annotations. By inferring per-step rewards through tree search rollouts using oracle final answers, the method enables self-training with accurate value signals that improve both policy and reward model performance. However, such approaches typically require a process reward model (PRM) to evaluate individual reasoning steps, which demands large-scale manual annotation of intermediate reasoning---a challenge that is especially severe in the GUI domain, where step-level reward annotation requires real devices or simulators, making it significantly more costly than text-based tasks.

Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO)~\cite{cpo} leveraged the intrinsic preference structure of tree-of-thought search trees to construct pairwise preference data without external rewards or human annotations. By treating reasoning steps on the final search path as preferred and pruned steps as dispreferred, CPO trained LLMs via DPO to align with optimal reasoning trajectories, achieving performance comparable to tree search while maintaining low inference latency.

Our work, MobileIPL, differs from the above approaches in several key aspects. Unlike ReST-MCTS*~\cite{restmcts}, which requires a separately trained PRM, we construct a CoaT-tree through iterative sampling and use rule-based rewards with backward credit assignment to derive thinking-level preference pairs directly---without manual step annotation. Compared to CPO~\cite{cpo} and the MCTS-based framework of Xie et al.~\cite{mcts-dpo}, which focus on text-based reasoning tasks, MobileIPL operates in the visually grounded mobile GUI domain, where reasoning steps involve multi-turn dialogues conditioned on real UI screenshots. Furthermore, unlike existing self-training methods that rely solely on final-answer correctness, our approach evaluates both the final action and the quality of intermediate reasoning steps, providing fine-grained optimization signals through Thinking-level DPO (T-DPO).

references.title: Android in the Wild: A Large-Scale Dataset for Android Device Control

s2_metadata.title: Android in the Wild: A Large-Scale Dataset for Android Device Control

tldr.text: [research paper] AITW introduces a large-scale, real-world Android device-control dataset with 715k episodes and 30k unique high-level natural language instructions across four Android versions and eight device types. Unlike prior datasets that rely on synthetic UI hierarchies or step-by-step element references, AITW captures raw screen observations and multi-step execution paths derived from human demonstrations, enabling systems to infer actions from visual context and generalize to novel apps, UIs, and platform versions. The dataset emphasizes real-world task diversity, including gestures like horizontal scrolls, and supports evaluation of robustness via hindsight relabeling and cross-version generalization.

@inproceedings{sun-etal-2024-determlr,
 title = "{D}eterm{LR}: Augmenting {LLM}-based Logical Reasoning from Indeterminacy to Determinacy",
 author = "Sun, Hongda and
 Xu, Weikai and
 Liu, Wei and
 Luan, Jian and
 Wang, Bin and
 Shang, Shuo and
 Wen, Ji-Rong and
 Yan, Rui",
 editor = "Ku, Lun-Wei and
 Martins, Andre and
 Srikumar, Vivek",
 booktitle = "Proceedings of the 62nd Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)",
 month = aug,
 year = "2024",
 address = "Bangkok, Thailand",
 publisher = "Association for Computational Linguistics",
 url = "https://aclanthology.org/2024.acl-long.531/",
 doi = "10.18653/v1/2024.acl-long.531",
 pages = "9828--9862",
 abstract = "Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized the landscape of reasoning tasks. To enhance the capabilities of LLMs to emulate human reasoning, prior studies have focused on modeling reasoning steps using various thought structures like chains, trees, or graphs. However, LLM-based reasoning still encounters the following challenges: (1) Limited adaptability of preset structures to diverse tasks; (2) Insufficient precision in exploiting known conditions to derive new ones; and (3) Inadequate consideration of historical reasoning experiences for subsequent reasoning steps. To this end, we propose DetermLR, a novel perspective that rethinks the reasoning process as an evolution from indeterminacy to determinacy. First, we categorize known conditions into two types: determinate and indeterminate premises, facilitating the transformation process. Subsequently, we leverage quantitative measurements to prioritize more relevant premises to explore new insights. Furthermore, we automate the storage and extraction of available premises and reasoning paths with reasoning memory, preserving historical reasoning details for subsequent reasoning steps. Comprehensive experimental results demonstrate that DetermLR surpasses all baselines on various logical reasoning benchmarks: LogiQA, ProofWriter, FOLIO, PrOntoQA, and LogicalDeduction. Compared to previous multi-step reasoning methods, DetermLR achieves higher accuracy with fewer reasoning steps, highlighting its superior efficiency and effectiveness in solving logical reasoning tasks."
}

@inproceedings{Luoling,
author = {Tan, Tao and Tu, Quan and Wu, Songhao and Sun, Hongda and Cheng, Chuanqi and Xu, Weikai and Yan, Rui},
title = {Luoling: An Immersive Cross-Modal Interactive Poem Creation Factory through Multi-Agent Collaboration},
year = {2025},
isbn = {9798400713316},
publisher = {Association for Computing Machinery},
address = {New York, NY, USA},
url = {https://doi.org/10.1145/3701716.3715184},
doi = {10.1145/3701716.3715184},
abstract = {Chinese poetry creation holds significant cultural and artistic value, but traditional methods are limited by attribute, text-only, or image-only inputs, often failing to capture the creator's detailed intents. Besides, the single-step generation will result in inconsistencies between the intents and the generated poem. In this work, we introduce Luoling, an immersive, cross-modal interactive poem creation factory with fine-grained, controllable attributes, driven by a multi-agent collaboration system. Luoling integrates text, image, and attribute fusion as inputs, enabling a richer, more expressive creative process. The interactive poem creation process is first introduced, where a large language model engages in proactive dialogue with the creator, guiding precise intent expression. Luoling is powered by a multi-agent collaboration system, including a poem writer, adviser, and rhythm checker, to refine the poem. Finally, the poem is delivered with the poet's tone using style-based text-to-speech technology, providing an authentic auditory experience. Luoling offers a more immersive environment, motivating creators to express their physical and spiritual feelings through compositions.},
booktitle = {Companion Proceedings of the ACM on Web Conference 2025},
pages = {2911–2914},
numpages = {4},
keywords = {cross-modal learning, multi-agent collaboration, poem creation},
location = {Sydney NSW, Australia},
series = {WWW '25}
}

sentences.text: With the rapid development of vision-language models (VLMs), researchers build mobile GUI agents and multi-agent frameworks based on closed-source VLMs.
Meanwhile, some researchers focus on training agents with stronger element grounding , page navigation , GUI understanding and task planning capabilities based on open-source VLMs.
Specifically, ReFT adopts reinforcement learning as a fine-tuning paradigm to improve performance on math problems.
ReST-MCTS* focuses on the higher-quality step reward, where the process reward model is important.
Xie, et al. label the preference via MCTS based on feedback from self-evaluation.
For mobile GUI agents, Digirl and Distrl use online trajectory collection to improve the generalization of agents whose process is very slow.
Reachagent uses DPO training to compare the quality of multiple actions.
TCPO also optimizes thoughts, but does not explicitly enforce thought–action consistency.
TreePO , TreeRL , and SPO segment long sequences into many short segments, which leads to high computational cost and low data efficiency.
In contrast, our method models thoughts with a fixed CoaT-tree and uses T-DPO to optimize the thinking process, while step values are computed directly from rule-based rewards, without unstable PRMs.

This design yields more efficient sampling and training, especially in GUI-agent settings.

references.label: achiam2023gpt
references.title: GPT-4 Technical Report

s2_metadata.title: GPT-4 Technical Report
s2_metadata.abstract: We report the development of GPT-4, a large-scale, multimodal model which can accept image and text inputs and produce text outputs. While less capable than humans in many real-world scenarios, GPT-4 exhibits human-level performance on various professional and academic benchmarks, including passing a simulated bar exam with a score around the top 10% of test takers. GPT-4 is a Transformer-based model pre-trained to predict the next token in a document. The post-training alignment process results in improved performance on measures of factuality and adherence to desired behavior. A core component of this project was developing infrastructure and optimization methods that behave predictably across a wide range of scales. This allowed us to accurately predict some aspects of GPT-4's performance based on models trained with no more than 1/1,000th the compute of GPT-4.

tldr.text: [technical report] GPT-4 is a multimodal Transformer model that achieves human-level performance on professional benchmarks like the simulated bar exam and outperforms prior models on MMLU and translated variants. A key innovation is the development of scalable deep learning infrastructure enabling accurate prediction of final loss and coding capability (e.g., HumanEval pass rate) from smaller, lower-compute models via power-law scaling laws, despite observed inverse scaling on specific tasks like Hindsight Neglect, which reveals non-monotonic behavior in certain cognitive tasks under scale.

references.label: xu2025llm
references.title: TPTU: Large Language Model-based AI Agents for Task Planning and Tool Usage

s2_metadata.title: TPTU: Large Language Model-based AI Agents for Task Planning and Tool Usage
s2_metadata.abstract: With recent advancements in natural language processing, Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as powerful tools for various real-world applications. Despite their prowess, the intrinsic generative abilities of LLMs may prove insufficient for handling complex tasks which necessitate a combination of task planning and the usage of external tools. In this paper, we first propose a structured framework tailored for LLM-based AI Agents and discuss the crucial capabilities necessary for tackling intricate problems. Within this framework, we design two distinct types of agents (i.e., one-step agent and sequential agent) to execute the inference process. Subsequently, we instantiate the framework using various LLMs and evaluate their Task Planning and Tool Usage (TPTU) abilities on typical tasks. By highlighting key findings and challenges, our goal is to provide a helpful resource for researchers and practitioners to leverage the power of LLMs in their AI applications. Our study emphasizes the substantial potential of these models, while also identifying areas that need more investigation and improvement.

tldr.text: [research paper] The paper proposes a structured framework for evaluating LLM-based AI Agents' Task Planning and Tool Usage (TPTU), introducing two agent types—one-step and sequential—to handle complex tasks through task decomposition and tool interaction. Empirical results reveal key limitations: format inconsistency, poor task understanding, tool overuse, and lack of summarization, highlighting critical gaps in current LLM agent capabilities for real-world deployment.

s2_metadata.title: Mobile-Bench-v2: A More Realistic and Comprehensive Benchmark for VLM-based Mobile Agents

tldr.text: [research paper] Mobile-Bench-v2 introduces a slot-based instruction generation method to create 12,856 realistic mobile task instructions from Mobile3M's graph-structured corpus, enabling multi-path offline evaluation with step-level rewards. It introduces noisy environments via pop-up/ads-rich apps and AITZ-Noise contamination, and an ambiguous instruction split with Q&A interactions to assess proactive agent behavior, addressing instability, lack of noise, and single-path limitations in prior benchmarks.

references.raw_text:
@inproceedings{sun-etal-2024-determlr,
 title = "{D}eterm{LR}: Augmenting {LLM}-based Logical Reasoning from Indeterminacy to Determinacy",
 author = "Sun, Hongda and
 Xu, Weikai and
 Liu, Wei and
 Luan, Jian and
 Wang, Bin and
 Shang, Shuo and
 Wen, Ji-Rong and
 Yan, Rui",
 editor = "Ku, Lun-Wei and
 Martins, Andre and
 Srikumar, Vivek",
 booktitle = "Proceedings of the 62nd Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)",
 month = aug,
 year = "2024",
 address = "Bangkok, Thailand",
 publisher = "Association for Computational Linguistics",
 url = "https://aclanthology.org/2024.acl-long.531/",
 doi = "10.18653/v1/2024.acl-long.531",
 pages = "9828--9862",
 abstract = "Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized the landscape of reasoning tasks. To enhance the capabilities of LLMs to emulate human reasoning, prior studies have focused on modeling reasoning steps using various thought structures like chains, trees, or graphs. However, LLM-based reasoning still encounters the following challenges: (1) Limited adaptability of preset structures to diverse tasks; (2) Insufficient precision in exploiting known conditions to derive new ones; and (3) Inadequate consideration of historical reasoning experiences for subsequent reasoning steps. To this end, we propose DetermLR, a novel perspective that rethinks the reasoning process as an evolution from indeterminacy to determinacy. First, we categorize known conditions into two types: determinate and indeterminate premises, facilitating the transformation process. Subsequently, we leverage quantitative measurements to prioritize more relevant premises to explore new insights. Furthermore, we automate the storage and extraction of available premises and reasoning paths with reasoning memory, preserving historical reasoning details for subsequent reasoning steps. Comprehensive experimental results demonstrate that DetermLR surpasses all baselines on various logical reasoning benchmarks: LogiQA, ProofWriter, FOLIO, PrOntoQA, and LogicalDeduction. Compared to previous multi-step reasoning methods, DetermLR achieves higher accuracy with fewer reasoning steps, highlighting its superior efficiency and effectiveness in solving logical reasoning tasks."
}
references.label: sun-etal-2024-determlr
references.title: Exotic Doubly Charmed Ds0*(2317)D and Ds1*(2460)D* Molecules

s2_metadata.title: Exotic Doubly Charmed Ds0*(2317)D and Ds1*(2460)D* Molecules
s2_metadata.abstract: The $D_{s0}^*(2317) D$ and $D_{s1}^*(2460) D^*$ heavy meson systems can exchange a kaon that is emitted in S-wave owing to the opposite intrinsic parity of the $D_{s0}^*$($D_{s1}^*$) and $D$($D^*$) mesons. As a consequence of the mass difference of the $D_{s0}^*$($D_{s1}^*$) and $D$($D^*$) mesons, the range of the kaon exchange potential will be significantly longer than expected, corresponding to an effective mass of about $200\\,{\\rm MeV}$. The potential will be very strong: the strength of the interaction is proportional to $(m_{D_{s0}} - m_D)^2 / f_{\\pi}^2$ and $(m_{D_{s1}} - m_{D^*})^2 / f_{\\pi}^2$. This combination of range and strength almost guarantees the existence of $D_{s0}^*(2317) D$ and $D_{s1}^*(2460) D^*$ bound states with $J^P = 0^{-}$ and $J^P = 0^{-}, 2^{-}$ respectively. Concrete calculations indicate a binding energy of $5-15\\,{\\rm MeV}$ independently of $J^P$. The $D_{s0}^*(2317) D$ and $D_{s1}^*(2460) D^*$ molecules have manifestly exotic flavour quantum numbers: $C=2$, $S=1$ and $I=1/2$. We expect the existence of bottom counterparts composed of the $B B_{s0}$ and $B^* B_{s1}^*$ mesons, which will be more bound and have a richer spectrum that might include a shallow P-wave state and an excited S-wave state.

tldr.text: [theoretical physics / hadron spectroscopy] The $D_{s0}^{*}(2317)D$ and $D_{s1}^{*}(2460)D^{*}$ systems are predicted to form bound molecular states due to long-range kaon exchange mediated by opposite intrinsic parities of the mesons, resulting in a strong, non-singular potential with effective mass ~200 MeV and binding energy of 5–15 MeV. The interaction strength scales as $(m_{D_{s}} - m_{D})^2 / f_{\pi}^2$, enabling robust predictions without short-range cutoffs, and the states exhibit exotic quantum numbers $C=2$, $S=1$, $I=1/2$. Bottom-sector counterparts ($BB_{s0}^*, B^*B_{s1}^*$) are expected to be more bound and may host P- and excited S-wave states.

s2_metadata.title: CoME: Empowering Channel-of-Mobile-Experts with Informative Hybrid-Capabilities Reasoning

tldr.text: [research paper] CoME introduces a novel output-oriented activation architecture for mobile agents, decoupling hybrid-capabilities reasoning into four specialized experts (screen summary, subtask planning, action decision, action function). It employs a progressive training strategy—Expert-FT, Router-FT, and CoT-FT—to achieve capability decoupling, stage-aligned expert activation, and balanced collaboration. InfoGain-Driven DPO mitigates error propagation by evaluating intermediate steps via information gain, enabling more informative and robust reasoning across complex mobile tasks.

references.label: lieffects
references.title: On the Effects of Data Scale on UI Control Agents

s2_metadata.title: On the Effects of Data Scale on UI Control Agents
s2_metadata.abstract: Autonomous agents that control computer interfaces to accomplish human tasks are emerging. Leveraging LLMs to power such agents has been of special interest, but unless fine-tuned on human-collected task demonstrations, performance is still relatively low. In this work we study whether fine-tuning alone is a viable approach for building real-world computer control agents. In particularly, we investigate how performance measured on both high and low-level tasks in domain and out of domain scales as more training data is collected. To this end we collect and release a new dataset, AndroidControl, consisting of 15,283 demonstrations of everyday tasks with Android apps. Compared to existing datasets, each AndroidControl task instance includes both high and low-level human-generated instructions, allowing us to explore the level of task complexity an agent can handle. Moreover, AndroidControl is the most diverse computer control dataset to date, including 14,548 unique tasks over 833 Android apps, thus allowing us to conduct in-depth analysis of the model performance in and out of the domain of the training data. Using the dataset, we find that when tested in domain fine-tuned models outperform zero and few-shot baselines and scale in such a way that robust performance might feasibly be obtained simply by collecting more data. Out of domain, performance scales significantly more slowly and suggests that in particular for high-level tasks, fine-tuning on more data alone may be insufficient for achieving robust out-of-domain performance.

tldr.text: [empirical study with dataset construction and scalability analysis] AndroidControl introduces a large-scale, diverse dataset of 15,283 human demonstrations of Android app tasks, each annotated with both high-level and low-level instructions. The study demonstrates that fine-tuned UI control agents scale effectively in-domain, with performance improving linearly with data volume, but exhibit significantly slower out-of-domain scaling—especially for high-level tasks—suggesting that data alone is insufficient for robust generalization beyond training distribution; this implies the need for additional techniques like task decomposition or domain adaptation for real-world deployment.

references.label: wen2023empowering
references.title: The Power of Smartphones

s2_metadata.title: The Power of Smartphones
s2_metadata.abstract: Smartphones have been shipped with multiple wireless network interfaces in order to meet their diverse communication and networking demands. However, as smartphones increasingly rely on wireless network connections to realize more functions, the demand of energy has been significantly increased, which has become the limit for people to explore smartphones' real power. In this paper, we first review typical smartphone computing systems, energy consumption of smartphone, and state-of-the-art techniques of energy saving for smartphones. Then we propose a location-assisted Wi-Fi discovery scheme, which discovers the nearest Wi-Fi network access points (APs) by using the user's location information. This allows the user to switch to the Wi-Fi interface in an intelligent manner when he/she arrives at the nearest Wi-Fi network AP. Thus we can meet the user's bandwidth needs and provide the best connectivity. Additionally, it avoids the long periods in idle state and greatly reduces the number of unnecessary Wi-Fi scans on the mobile device. Our experiments and simulations demonstrate that our scheme effectively saves energy for smartphones integrated with Wi-Fi and cellular interfaces.

tldr.text: [research paper] A location-assisted Wi-Fi discovery scheme leverages user location (via A-GPS or ZigBee) to intelligently switch to the nearest Wi-Fi access point, eliminating unnecessary Wi-Fi scans and reducing idle power consumption. The approach minimizes energy waste by aligning network interface selection with user proximity, achieving significant energy savings without compromising connectivity, validated through simulations and experiments.

references.label: liu2025mobilesteward
references.title: MobileSteward: Integrating Multiple App-Oriented Agents with Self-Evolution to Automate Cross-App Instructions

s2_metadata.title: MobileSteward: Integrating Multiple App-Oriented Agents with Self-Evolution to Automate Cross-App Instructions
s2_metadata.abstract: Mobile phone agents can assist people in automating daily tasks on their phones, which have emerged as a pivotal research spotlight. However, existing procedure-oriented agents struggle with cross-app instructions, due to the following challenges: (1) complex task relationships, (2) diverse app environment, and (3) error propagation and information loss in multi-step execution. Drawing inspiration from object-oriented programming principles, we recognize that object-oriented solutions is more suitable for cross-app instruction. To address these challenges, we propose a self-evolving multi-agent framework named MobileSteward, which integrates multiple app-oriented StaffAgents coordinated by a centralized StewardAgent. We design three specialized modules in MobileSteward: (1) Dynamic Recruitment generates a scheduling graph guided by information flow to explicitly associate tasks among apps. (2) Assigned Execution assigns the task to app-oriented StaffAgents, each equipped with app-specialized expertise to address the diversity between apps. (3) Adjusted Evaluation conducts evaluation to provide reflection tips or deliver key information, which alleviates error propagation and information loss during multi-step execution. To continuously improve the performance of MobileSteward, we develop a Memory-based Self-evolution mechanism, which summarizes the experience from successful execution, to improve the performance of MobileSteward. We establish the first English Cross-APP Benchmark (CAPBench) in the real-world environment to evaluate the agents' capabilities of solving complex cross-app instructions. Experimental results demonstrate that MobileSteward achieves the best performance compared to both single-agent and multi-agent frameworks, highlighting the superiority of MobileSteward in better handling user instructions with diverse complexity.

tldr.text: [research paper] MobileSteward introduces an object-oriented multi-agent framework for cross-app instruction automation, where a centralized StewardAgent coordinates app-specialized StaffAgents via dynamic task scheduling, specialized execution, and adaptive evaluation. The framework overcomes challenges of complex task dependencies, app heterogeneity, and error propagation through a memory-based self-evolution mechanism that learns and refines agent expertise and task guidelines from successful executions, outperforming both single-agent and conventional multi-agent approaches on a newly proposed real-world benchmark (CAPBench).

references.label: chen2025step
references.title: STEP: Success-Rate-Aware Trajectory-Efficient Policy Optimization

s2_metadata.title: STEP: Success-Rate-Aware Trajectory-Efficient Policy Optimization

tldr.text: [research paper] STEP introduces a success-rate-aware, step-level policy optimization framework that dynamically resamples trajectories based on per-task success rates, decomposes only successful trajectories into step-level samples for accurate credit assignment, and applies step-level GRPO augmentation to low-success tasks. This addresses uniform sampling, inaccurate credit assignment, and inefficient sample collection in multi-turn RL by enabling adaptive resource allocation and fine-grained learning, resulting in faster convergence and better generalization under limited sampling budgets.

references.label: yang2023appagent
references.title: AppAgent: Multimodal Agents as Smartphone Users

s2_metadata.title: AppAgent: Multimodal Agents as Smartphone Users
s2_metadata.venue: International Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
s2_metadata.abstract: Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have led to the creation of intelligent agents capable of performing complex tasks. This paper introduces a novel LLM-based multimodal agent framework designed to operate smartphone applications. Our framework enables the agent to operate smartphone applications through a simplified action space, mimicking human-like interactions such as tapping and swiping. This novel approach bypasses the need for system back-end access, thereby broadening its applicability across diverse apps. Central to our agent's functionality is its innovative learning method. The agent learns to navigate and use new apps either through autonomous exploration or by observing human demonstrations. This process generates a knowledge base that the agent refers to for executing complex tasks across different applications. To demonstrate the practicality of our agent, we conducted extensive testing over 50 tasks in 10 different applications, including social media, email, maps, shopping, and sophisticated image editing tools. The results affirm our agent's proficiency in handling a diverse array of high-level tasks.

tldr.text: [research paper] AppAgent introduces a multimodal LLM-based agent that operates smartphone apps via human-like GUI interactions (tapping/swiping) without system-level access, enabling cross-app generalization through autonomous exploration or human demonstration. The agent builds a task-specific knowledge base from interactions, avoiding retraining or extensive app-specific data collection, and achieves high performance across 50 tasks in 10 diverse apps including image editing and social media, demonstrating robust adaptability to novel and dynamic UIs.

references.label: zheng2024gpt
references.title: GPT-4V(ision) is a Generalist Web Agent, if Grounded

s2_metadata.title: GPT-4V(ision) is a Generalist Web Agent, if Grounded
s2_metadata.abstract: The recent development on large multimodal models (LMMs), especially GPT-4V(ision) and Gemini, has been quickly expanding the capability boundaries of multimodal models beyond traditional tasks like image captioning and visual question answering. In this work, we explore the potential of LMMs like GPT-4V as a generalist web agent that can follow natural language instructions to complete tasks on any given website. We propose SEEACT, a generalist web agent that harnesses the power of LMMs for integrated visual understanding and acting on the web. We evaluate on the recent MIND2WEB benchmark. In addition to standard offline evaluation on cached websites, we enable a new online evaluation setting by developing a tool that allows running web agents on live websites. We show that GPT-4V presents a great potential for web agents -- it can successfully complete 51.1 of the tasks on live websites if we manually ground its textual plans into actions on the websites. This substantially outperforms text-only LLMs like GPT-4 or smaller models (FLAN-T5 and BLIP-2) specifically fine-tuned for web agents. However, grounding still remains a major challenge. Existing LMM grounding strategies like set-of-mark prompting turns out to be not effective for web agents, and the best grounding strategy we develop in this paper leverages both the HTML structure and visuals. Yet, there is still a substantial gap with oracle grounding, leaving ample room for further improvement. All code, data, and evaluation tools are available at https://github.com/OSU-NLP-Group/SeeAct.

tldr.text: [research paper] SEEACT demonstrates that GPT-4V can act as a generalist web agent by generating accurate textual plans for complex web tasks, achieving 51.1% success on live websites when grounded with oracle-level element and operation selection. However, grounding remains suboptimal—current methods suffer from hallucination and a 20–30% gap versus oracle performance—due to the lack of effective fusion between visual semantics and HTML structure. The best grounding strategy combines visual and textual cues from HTML elements, outperforming image-only prompting by up to 10%, and reveals that in-context learning generalizes better than supervised fine-tuning across unseen websites.

references.label: qin2025ui
references.title: UI-TARS: Pioneering Automated GUI Interaction with Native Agents

s2_metadata.title: UI-TARS: Pioneering Automated GUI Interaction with Native Agents
s2_metadata.abstract: This paper introduces UI-TARS, a native GUI agent model that solely perceives the screenshots as input and performs human-like interactions (e.g., keyboard and mouse operations). Unlike prevailing agent frameworks that depend on heavily wrapped commercial models (e.g., GPT-4o) with expert-crafted prompts and workflows, UI-TARS is an end-to-end model that outperforms these sophisticated frameworks. Experiments demonstrate its superior performance: UI-TARS achieves SOTA performance in 10+ GUI agent benchmarks evaluating perception, grounding, and GUI task execution. Notably, in the OSWorld benchmark, UI-TARS achieves scores of 24.6 with 50 steps and 22.7 with 15 steps, outperforming Claude (22.0 and 14.9 respectively). In AndroidWorld, UI-TARS achieves 46.6, surpassing GPT-4o (34.5). UI-TARS incorporates several key innovations: (1) Enhanced Perception: leveraging a large-scale dataset of GUI screenshots for context-aware understanding of UI elements and precise captioning; (2) Unified Action Modeling, which standardizes actions into a unified space across platforms and achieves precise grounding and interaction through large-scale action traces; (3) System-2 Reasoning, which incorporates deliberate reasoning into multi-step decision making, involving multiple reasoning patterns such as task decomposition, reflection thinking, milestone recognition, etc. (4) Iterative Training with Reflective Online Traces, which addresses the data bottleneck by automatically collecting, filtering, and reflectively refining new interaction traces on hundreds of virtual machines. Through iterative training and reflection tuning, UI-TARS continuously learns from its mistakes and adapts to unforeseen situations with minimal human intervention. We also analyze the evolution path of GUI agents to guide the further development of this domain.

tldr.text: [research paper] UI-TARS introduces a native GUI agent model that operates solely on screenshots, bypassing textual representations and modular frameworks. It achieves state-of-the-art performance across GUI benchmarks via enhanced perception from large-scale screenshot data, unified action modeling across platforms, System-2 reasoning for multi-step planning (task decomposition, reflection, milestone recognition), and iterative training using reflective online traces collected from virtual machines—enabling continuous self-improvement without human intervention. The model closes the data bottleneck in end-to-end GUI agents by synthesizing perception, reasoning, memory, and action into a single, data-driven architecture.

references.label: teamqwen2
references.title: Qwen2.5-VL Technical Report

s2_metadata.title: Qwen2.5-VL Technical Report
s2_metadata.abstract: We introduce Qwen2.5-VL, the latest flagship model of Qwen vision-language series, which demonstrates significant advancements in both foundational capabilities and innovative functionalities. Qwen2.5-VL achieves a major leap forward in understanding and interacting with the world through enhanced visual recognition, precise object localization, robust document parsing, and long-video comprehension. A standout feature of Qwen2.5-VL is its ability to localize objects using bounding boxes or points accurately. It provides robust structured data extraction from invoices, forms, and tables, as well as detailed analysis of charts, diagrams, and layouts. To handle complex inputs, Qwen2.5-VL introduces dynamic resolution processing and absolute time encoding, enabling it to process images of varying sizes and videos of extended durations (up to hours) with second-level event localization. This allows the model to natively perceive spatial scales and temporal dynamics without relying on traditional normalization techniques. By training a native dynamic-resolution Vision Transformer (ViT) from scratch and incorporating Window Attention, we reduce computational overhead while maintaining native resolution. As a result, Qwen2.5-VL excels not only in static image and document understanding but also as an interactive visual agent capable of reasoning, tool usage, and task execution in real-world scenarios such as operating computers and mobile devices. Qwen2.5-VL is available in three sizes, addressing diverse use cases from edge AI to high-performance computing. The flagship Qwen2.5-VL-72B model matches state-of-the-art models like GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet, particularly excelling in document and diagram understanding. Additionally, Qwen2.5-VL maintains robust linguistic performance, preserving the core language competencies of the Qwen2.5 LLM.

tldr.text: [research paper] Qwen2.5-VL introduces a native dynamic-resolution Vision Transformer with window attention and absolute time-aligned MROPE to enable high-fidelity, fine-grained visual perception at native input scales. It achieves superior performance in document parsing, object localization, and ultra-long video understanding by processing variable-sized images and hours-long videos with second-level temporal grounding, without relying on normalization or fixed resolutions. The model supports real-world agent functionality via precise spatial and temporal grounding, with strong generalization across domains and no task-specific fine-tuning required.

references.label: ding2024mobileagent
references.title: Mobile-Agent: Autonomous Multi-Modal Mobile Device Agent with Visual Perception

s2_metadata.title: Mobile-Agent: Autonomous Multi-Modal Mobile Device Agent with Visual Perception
s2_metadata.abstract: Mobile device agent based on Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLM) is becoming a popular application. In this paper, we introduce Mobile-Agent, an autonomous multi-modal mobile device agent. Mobile-Agent first leverages visual perception tools to accurately identify and locate both the visual and textual elements within the app's front-end interface. Based on the perceived vision context, it then autonomously plans and decomposes the complex operation task, and navigates the mobile Apps through operations step by step. Different from previous solutions that rely on XML files of Apps or mobile system metadata, Mobile-Agent allows for greater adaptability across diverse mobile operating environments in a vision-centric way, thereby eliminating the necessity for system-specific customizations. To assess the performance of Mobile-Agent, we introduced Mobile-Eval, a benchmark for evaluating mobile device operations. Based on Mobile-Eval, we conducted a comprehensive evaluation of Mobile-Agent. The experimental results indicate that Mobile-Agent achieved remarkable accuracy and completion rates. Even with challenging instructions, such as multi-app operations, Mobile-Agent can still complete the requirements. Code and model will be open-sourced at https://github.com/X-PLUG/MobileAgent.

tldr.text: [research paper] Mobile-Agent introduces a vision-centric, system-agnostic mobile device agent that uses visual perception (text and icon detection) to localize operations on screen without relying on app XML or system metadata. It leverages GPT-4V for task planning and self-reflection, enabling autonomous execution of complex, multi-app operations through real-time screenshot analysis; evaluated via Mobile-Eval, a benchmark with 10 apps and three difficulty levels, showing high completion accuracy even under ambiguous or multi-step instructions.

references.label: li2024appagent
references.title: AppAgent: Multimodal Agents as Smartphone Users

s2_metadata.title: AppAgent: Multimodal Agents as Smartphone Users
s2_metadata.venue: International Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
s2_metadata.abstract: Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have led to the creation of intelligent agents capable of performing complex tasks. This paper introduces a novel LLM-based multimodal agent framework designed to operate smartphone applications. Our framework enables the agent to operate smartphone applications through a simplified action space, mimicking human-like interactions such as tapping and swiping. This novel approach bypasses the need for system back-end access, thereby broadening its applicability across diverse apps. Central to our agent's functionality is its innovative learning method. The agent learns to navigate and use new apps either through autonomous exploration or by observing human demonstrations. This process generates a knowledge base that the agent refers to for executing complex tasks across different applications. To demonstrate the practicality of our agent, we conducted extensive testing over 50 tasks in 10 different applications, including social media, email, maps, shopping, and sophisticated image editing tools. The results affirm our agent's proficiency in handling a diverse array of high-level tasks.

tldr.text: [research paper] AppAgent introduces a multimodal LLM-based agent that operates smartphone apps via human-like GUI interactions (tapping/swiping) without system-level access, enabling cross-app generalization through autonomous exploration or human demonstration. The agent builds a task-specific knowledge base from interactions, avoiding retraining or extensive app-specific data collection, and achieves high performance across 50 tasks in 10 diverse apps including image editing and social media, demonstrating robust adaptability to novel and dynamic UIs.

references.label: wang2024mobile
references.title: Mobile-Agent-v2: Mobile Device Operation Assistant with Effective Navigation via Multi-Agent Collaboration

s2_metadata.title: Mobile-Agent-v2: Mobile Device Operation Assistant with Effective Navigation via Multi-Agent Collaboration
s2_metadata.abstract: Mobile device operation tasks are increasingly becoming a popular multi-modal AI application scenario. Current Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs), constrained by their training data, lack the capability to function effectively as operation assistants. Instead, MLLM-based agents, which enhance capabilities through tool invocation, are gradually being applied to this scenario. However, the two major navigation challenges in mobile device operation tasks, task progress navigation and focus content navigation, are significantly complicated under the single-agent architecture of existing work. This is due to the overly long token sequences and the interleaved text-image data format, which limit performance. To address these navigation challenges effectively, we propose Mobile-Agent-v2, a multi-agent architecture for mobile device operation assistance. The architecture comprises three agents: planning agent, decision agent, and reflection agent. The planning agent generates task progress, making the navigation of history operations more efficient. To retain focus content, we design a memory unit that updates with task progress. Additionally, to correct erroneous operations, the reflection agent observes the outcomes of each operation and handles any mistakes accordingly. Experimental results indicate that Mobile-Agent-v2 achieves over a 30% improvement in task completion compared to the single-agent architecture of Mobile-Agent. The code is open-sourced at https://github.com/X-PLUG/MobileAgent.

tldr.text: [research paper] Mobile-Agent-v2 introduces a three-agent collaborative architecture—planning, decision, and reflection agents—to resolve navigation challenges in mobile device operation tasks. The planning agent compresses interleaved image-text operation histories into pure-text task progress, reducing context length; a dedicated memory unit preserves focus content from past screens for contextual retrieval; the reflection agent detects and corrects erroneous operations by comparing pre- and post-operation screen states, enabling error recovery and improved task completion by over 30% compared to single-agent baselines.

references.label: Luoling
references.title: Youling: an AI-Assisted Lyrics Creation System

s2_metadata.title: Youling: an AI-Assisted Lyrics Creation System
s2_metadata.venue: Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing
s2_metadata.abstract: Recently, a variety of neural models have been proposed for lyrics generation. However, most previous work completes the generation process in a single pass with little human intervention. We believe that lyrics creation is a creative process with human intelligence centered. AI should play a role as an assistant in the lyrics creation process, where human interactions are crucial for high-quality creation. This paper demonstrates \\textit{Youling}, an AI-assisted lyrics creation system, designed to collaborate with music creators. In the lyrics generation process, \\textit{Youling} supports traditional one pass full-text generation mode as well as an interactive generation mode, which allows users to select the satisfactory sentences from generated candidates conditioned on preceding context. The system also provides a revision module which enables users to revise undesired sentences or words of lyrics repeatedly. Besides, \\textit{Youling} allows users to use multifaceted attributes to control the content and format of generated lyrics. The demo video of the system is available at https://youtu.be/DFeNpHk0pm4.

tldr.text: [research paper] Youling introduces an interactive, human-centered AI-assisted lyrics creation system that supports both full-text and line-by-line interactive generation, enabling users to select, revise, and refine lyrics iteratively. It leverages a GPT-2-based Transformer model pre-trained on 30GB of Chinese novels and fine-tuned on 300K songs with annotated style, emotion, and keyword metadata, using structured controlling attributes (e.g., text style, emotion, theme, rhyme, line count) to guide generation. The system uniquely combines controllable format and content attributes with a revision module, allowing for iterative refinement and human-in-the-loop creativity, marking a shift from passive generation to active collaboration in music creation.

references.label: cheng2024seeclick
references.title: SeeClick: Harnessing GUI Grounding for Advanced Visual GUI Agents

s2_metadata.title: SeeClick: Harnessing GUI Grounding for Advanced Visual GUI Agents
s2_metadata.venue: Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics
s2_metadata.abstract: Graphical User Interface (GUI) agents are designed to automate complex tasks on digital devices, such as smartphones and desktops. Most existing GUI agents interact with the environment through extracted structured data, which can be notably lengthy (e.g., HTML) and occasionally inaccessible (e.g., on desktops). To alleviate this issue, we propose a novel visual GUI agent -- SeeClick, which only relies on screenshots for task automation. In our preliminary study, we have discovered a key challenge in developing visual GUI agents: GUI grounding -- the capacity to accurately locate screen elements based on instructions. To tackle this challenge, we propose to enhance SeeClick with GUI grounding pre-training and devise a method to automate the curation of GUI grounding data. Along with the efforts above, we have also created ScreenSpot, the first realistic GUI grounding benchmark that encompasses mobile, desktop, and web environments. After pre-training, SeeClick demonstrates significant improvement in ScreenSpot over various baselines. Moreover, comprehensive evaluations on three widely used benchmarks consistently support our finding that advancements in GUI grounding directly correlate with enhanced performance in downstream GUI agent tasks. The model, data and code are available at https://github.com/njucckevin/SeeClick.

tldr.text: [research paper] SeeClick introduces a visual GUI agent that operates directly on screenshots across mobile, desktop, and web platforms, bypassing structured text dependencies. The core innovation is GUI grounding pre-training—enhancing Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) with explicit instruction-to-element localization via a novel, automated dataset curation pipeline. This enables accurate element localization (e.g., text, icons, widgets) in diverse GUI environments, and is validated through ScreenSpot, the first cross-platform grounding benchmark, showing that grounding performance directly correlates with downstream agent task success in MiniWob, AITW, and Mind2Web.

references.label: wu2024atlas
references.title: OS-ATLAS: A Foundation Action Model for Generalist GUI Agents

s2_metadata.title: OS-ATLAS: A Foundation Action Model for Generalist GUI Agents
s2_metadata.abstract: Existing efforts in building GUI agents heavily rely on the availability of robust commercial Vision-Language Models (VLMs) such as GPT-4o and GeminiProVision. Practitioners are often reluctant to use open-source VLMs due to their significant performance lag compared to their closed-source counterparts, particularly in GUI grounding and Out-Of-Distribution (OOD) scenarios. To facilitate future research in this area, we developed OS-Atlas - a foundational GUI action model that excels at GUI grounding and OOD agentic tasks through innovations in both data and modeling. We have invested significant engineering effort in developing an open-source toolkit for synthesizing GUI grounding data across multiple platforms, including Windows, Linux, MacOS, Android, and the web. Leveraging this toolkit, we are releasing the largest open-source cross-platform GUI grounding corpus to date, which contains over 13 million GUI elements. This dataset, combined with innovations in model training, provides a solid foundation for OS-Atlas to understand GUI screenshots and generalize to unseen interfaces. Through extensive evaluation across six benchmarks spanning three different platforms (mobile, desktop, and web), OS-Atlas demonstrates significant performance improvements over previous state-of-the-art models. Our evaluation also uncovers valuable insights into continuously improving and scaling the agentic capabilities of open-source VLMs.

tldr.text: [research paper] OS-Atlas introduces a foundational GUI action model that achieves superior GUI grounding and out-of-distribution generalization by addressing two critical bottlenecks: (1) lack of large-scale, multi-platform GUI screenshot data, and (2) inconsistent action naming across platforms. The paper presents a novel data synthesis toolkit enabling automated cross-platform GUI data generation, curates the largest open-source cross-platform GUI grounding corpus (13M+ elements across Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and web), and resolves action naming conflicts via unified labeling. OS-Atlas outperforms prior state-of-the-art models across six benchmarks spanning desktop, mobile, and web, demonstrating strong zero-shot generalization and serving as a viable open-source alternative to commercial VLMs like GPT-4o.

references.label: niu2024screenagent
references.title: ScreenAgent: A Vision Language Model-driven Computer Control Agent

s2_metadata.title: ScreenAgent: A Vision Language Model-driven Computer Control Agent
s2_metadata.venue: International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence
s2_metadata.abstract: Existing Large Language Models (LLM) can invoke a variety of tools and APIs to complete complex tasks. The computer, as the most powerful and universal tool, could potentially be controlled directly by a trained LLM agent. Powered by the computer, we can hopefully build a more generalized agent to assist humans in various daily digital works. In this paper, we construct an environment for a Vision Language Model (VLM) agent to interact with a real computer screen. Within this environment, the agent can observe screenshots and manipulate the Graphics User Interface (GUI) by outputting mouse and keyboard actions. We also design an automated control pipeline that includes planning, acting, and reflecting phases, guiding the agent to continuously interact with the environment and complete multi-step tasks. Additionally, we construct the ScreenAgent Dataset, which collects screenshots and action sequences when completing a variety of daily computer tasks. Finally, we trained a model, ScreenAgent, which achieved computer control capabilities comparable to GPT-4V and demonstrated more precise UI positioning capabilities. Our attempts could inspire further research on building a generalist LLM agent. The code is available at \\url{https://github.com/niuzaisheng/ScreenAgent}.

tldr.text: [research paper] ScreenAgent introduces a vision-language model-driven agent that directly controls real computer screens via VNC, enabling continuous GUI interaction through a planning-acting-reflection pipeline inspired by Kolb’s experiential learning cycle. The agent achieves precise UI positioning and comparable performance to GPT-4V in multi-step tasks, validated on a newly constructed ScreenAgent dataset with fine-grained evaluation metrics; it outperforms prior work by integrating reflective feedback loops and full tool invocation chains for autonomous, real-time screen manipulation.

references.label: lu2024gui
references.title: GUIOdyssey: A Comprehensive Dataset for Cross-App GUI Navigation on Mobile Devices

s2_metadata.title: GUIOdyssey: A Comprehensive Dataset for Cross-App GUI Navigation on Mobile Devices
s2_metadata.venue: IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision
s2_metadata.abstract: Autonomous Graphical User Interface (GUI) navigation agents can enhance user experience in communication, entertainment, and productivity by streamlining workflows and reducing manual intervention. However, prior GUI agents often trained with datasets comprising tasks that can be completed within a single app, leading to poor performance in cross-app navigation. To address this problem, we present GUIOdyssey, a comprehensive dataset for cross-app mobile GUI navigation. GUIOdyssey comprises 8,334 episodes with an average of 15.3 steps per episode, covering 6 mobile devices, 212 distinct apps, and 1,357 app combinations. Each step is enriched with detailed semantic reasoning annotations, which aid the model in building cognitive processes and enhancing its reasoning abilities for complex cross-app tasks. Building on GUIOdyssey, we develop OdysseyAgent, an exploratory multimodal agent for long-step cross-app navigation equipped with a history resampler module that efficiently attends to historical screenshot tokens, balancing performance and inference speed. Extensive experiments conducted in both in-domain and out-of-domain scenarios validate the effectiveness of our approach. Moreover, we demonstrate that historial information involving actions, screenshots and context in our dataset can significantly enhances OdysseyAgent's performance on complex cross-app tasks.

tldr.text: [research paper] GUIOdyssey introduces the first comprehensive cross-app mobile GUI navigation dataset with 8,334 episodes spanning 212 apps and 1,357 app combinations, featuring rich semantic annotations (screen comprehension, historical context, decision-making) generated via GPT-4o to support cognitive reasoning. OdysseyAgent, a multimodal agent with a history resampler module, efficiently processes long-step cross-app navigation by selectively attending to historical screenshot tokens, achieving state-of-the-art performance on both in-domain and out-of-domain tasks, with significant gains from incorporating semantic and historical context.

references.label: gou2024navigating
references.title: Navigating the Digital World as Humans Do: Universal Visual Grounding for GUI Agents

s2_metadata.title: Navigating the Digital World as Humans Do: Universal Visual Grounding for GUI Agents
s2_metadata.abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are transforming the capabilities of graphical user interface (GUI) agents, facilitating their transition from controlled simulations to complex, real-world applications across various platforms. However, the effectiveness of these agents hinges on the robustness of their grounding capability. Current GUI agents predominantly utilize text-based representations such as HTML or accessibility trees, which, despite their utility, often introduce noise, incompleteness, and increased computational overhead. In this paper, we advocate a human-like embodiment for GUI agents that perceive the environment entirely visually and directly perform pixel-level operations on the GUI. The key is visual grounding models that can accurately map diverse referring expressions of GUI elements to their coordinates on the GUI across different platforms. We show that a simple recipe, which includes web-based synthetic data and slight adaptation of the LLaVA architecture, is surprisingly effective for training such visual grounding models. We collect the largest dataset for GUI visual grounding so far, containing 10M GUI elements and their referring expressions over 1.3M screenshots, and use it to train UGround, a strong universal visual grounding model for GUI agents. Empirical results on six benchmarks spanning three categories (grounding, offline agent, and online agent) show that 1) UGround substantially outperforms existing visual grounding models for GUI agents, by up to 20% absolute, and 2) agents with UGround outperform state-of-the-art agents, despite the fact that existing agents use additional text-based input while ours only uses visual perception. These results provide strong support for the feasibility and promises of GUI agents that navigate the digital world as humans do.

tldr.text: [research paper] The paper introduces SeeAct-V, a framework for GUI agents with human-like visual embodiment that relies solely on pixel-level perception and operations, bypassing text-based representations like HTML or accessibility trees. A simple, effective training recipe—using web-synthetic data and a lightly adapted LLaVA architecture—enables the construction of UGround, the largest GUI visual grounding dataset (10M elements across 1.3M screenshots) and a universal grounding model. Empirical results show UGround outperforms existing methods by up to 20% in grounding accuracy and enables agents to match or exceed state-of-the-art performance in offline and online agent tasks without any text input, demonstrating the feasibility and practicality of fully visual GUI navigation.

references.label: wang2025fedmobileagent
references.title: FedMABench: Benchmarking Mobile Agents on Decentralized Heterogeneous User Data

s2_metadata.title: FedMABench: Benchmarking Mobile Agents on Decentralized Heterogeneous User Data
s2_metadata.abstract:
Mobile agents have attracted tremendous research participation recently. Traditional approaches to mobile agent training rely on centralized data collection, leading to high cost and limited scalability. Distributed training utilizing federated learning offers an alternative by harnessing real-world user data, providing scalability and reducing costs. However, pivotal challenges, including the absence of standardized benchmarks, hinder progress in this field.\
  To tackle the challenges, we introduce FedMABench, the first benchmark for federated training and evaluation of mobile agents, specifically designed for heterogeneous scenarios. FedMABench features 6 datasets with 30+ subsets, 8 federated algorithms, 10+ base models, and over 800 apps across 5 categories, providing a comprehensive framework for evaluating mobile agents across diverse environments. Through extensive experiments, we uncover several key insights: federated algorithms consistently outperform local training; the distribution of specific apps plays a crucial role in heterogeneity; and, even apps from distinct categories can exhibit correlations during training. FedMABench is publicly available at: https://github.com/wwh0411/FedMABench with the datasets at: https://huggingface.co/datasets/wwh0411/FedMABench.

tldr.text: [research paper] FedMABench introduces the first standardized benchmark for federated mobile agent training on decentralized, heterogeneous user data, featuring 6 datasets with 30+ subsets across 5 app categories and 8 federated algorithms. It systematically models three types of heterogeneity—app category distribution, specific app preferences, and two-level sample counts (episode and step counts)—revealing that federated training consistently outperforms local training, while app-level distribution and task complexity significantly impact agent performance. The benchmark enables rigorous, reproducible evaluation of mobile agents in real-world distributed settings with privacy-preserving, scalable training protocols.

references.label: you2024ferret
references.title: FERRET: Framework for Expansion Reliant Red Teaming

s2_metadata.title: FERRET: Framework for Expansion Reliant Red Teaming
s2_metadata.abstract: We introduce a multi-faceted automated red teaming framework in which the goal is to generate multi-modal adversarial conversations that would break a target model and introduce various expansions that would result in more effective and efficient adversarial conversations. The introduced expansions include: 1. Horizontal expansion in which the goal is for the red team model to self-improve and generate more effective conversation starters that would shape a conversation. 2. Vertical expansion in which the goal is to take these conversation starters that are discovered in the horizontal expansion phase and expand them into effective multi-modal conversations and 3. Meta expansion in which the goal is for the red team model to discover more effective multi-modal attack strategies during the course of a conversation. We call our framework FERRET (Framework for Expansion Reliant Red Teaming) and compare it with various existing automated red teaming approaches. In our experiments, we demonstrate the effectiveness of FERRET in generating effective multi-modal adversarial conversations and its superior performance against existing state of the art approaches.

tldr.text: [research paper] FERRET introduces a multi-dimensional automated red teaming framework that dynamically discovers and refines adversarial conversation starters (horizontal expansion), expands them into multi-modal, multi-turn conversations (vertical expansion), and evolves novel attack strategies through meta-expansion. By integrating text-image fusion and self-improving feedback loops, FERRET surpasses existing paradigms by jointly discovering attack goals and exploiting vulnerabilities in a scalable, adaptive manner without requiring predefined prompts or static attack strategies.
