references.label: baechler2024screenai
references.title: ScreenAI: A Vision-Language Model for UI and Infographics Understanding

s2_metadata.title: ScreenAI: A Vision-Language Model for UI and Infographics Understanding
s2_metadata.venue: International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence
s2_metadata.abstract: Screen user interfaces (UIs) and infographics, sharing similar visual language and design principles, play important roles in human communication and human-machine interaction. We introduce ScreenAI, a vision-language model that specializes in UI and infographics understanding. Our model improves upon the PaLI architecture with the flexible patching strategy of pix2struct and is trained on a unique mixture of datasets. At the heart of this mixture is a novel screen annotation task in which the model has to identify the type and location of UI elements. We use these text annotations to describe screens to Large Language Models and automatically generate question-answering (QA), UI navigation, and summarization training datasets at scale. We run ablation studies to demonstrate the impact of these design choices. At only 5B parameters, ScreenAI achieves new state-of-the-artresults on UI- and infographics-based tasks (Multi-page DocVQA, WebSRC, MoTIF and Widget Captioning), and new best-in-class performance on others (Chart QA, DocVQA, and InfographicVQA) compared to models of similar size. Finally, we release three new datasets: one focused on the screen annotation task and two others focused on question answering.

tldr.text: [research paper] ScreenAI introduces a vision-language model that unifies UI and infographic understanding by leveraging a novel screen annotation task to generate textual representations of interfaces, which are then used to train and scale question-answering, navigation, and summarization datasets via large language models. The model combines PaLI’s architecture with pix2struct’s flexible patching for adaptive image grid processing, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple UI and infographic benchmarks at just 5B parameters, with new best-in-class performance on Chart QA, DocVQA, and InfographicVQA, and releases three new datasets: Screen Annotation, ScreenQA Short, and Complex ScreenQA.

references.label: zhang2024llamatouch
references.title: LlamaTouch: A Faithful and Scalable Testbed for Mobile UI Task Automation

s2_metadata.title: LlamaTouch: A Faithful and Scalable Testbed for Mobile UI Task Automation
s2_metadata.venue: ACM Symposium on User Interface Software and Technology
s2_metadata.abstract: The emergent large language/multimodal models facilitate the evolution of mobile agents, especially in mobile UI task automation. However, existing evaluation approaches, which rely on human validation or established datasets to compare agent-predicted actions with predefined action sequences, are unscalable and unfaithful. To overcome these limitations, this paper presents LlamaTouch, a testbed for on-device mobile UI task execution and faithful, scalable task evaluation. By observing that the task execution process only transfers UI states, LlamaTouch employs a novel evaluation approach that only assesses whether an agent traverses all manually annotated, essential application/system states. LlamaTouch comprises three key techniques: (1) On-device task execution that enables mobile agents to interact with realistic mobile environments for task execution. (2) Fine-grained UI component annotation that merges pixel-level screenshots and textual screen hierarchies to explicitly identify and precisely annotate essential UI components with a rich set of designed annotation primitives. (3) A multi-level application state matching algorithm that utilizes exact and fuzzy matching to accurately detect critical information in each screen, even with unpredictable UI layout/content dynamics. LlamaTouch currently incorporates four mobile agents and 496 tasks, encompassing both tasks in the widely-used datasets and our self-constructed ones to cover more diverse mobile applications. Evaluation results demonstrate LlamaTouch's high faithfulness of evaluation in real-world mobile environments and its better scalability than human validation. LlamaTouch also enables easy task annotation and integration of new mobile agents. Code and dataset are publicly available at https://github.com/LlamaTouch/LlamaTouch.

tldr.text: [research paper] LlamaTouch introduces a novel evaluation framework for mobile UI task automation that replaces action-sequence matching with state-based verification, enabling faithful and scalable assessment in real-world environments. It uses fine-grained UI annotations combining pixel-level screenshots and semantic screen hierarchies to identify essential application states, and employs a multi-level matching algorithm (exact and fuzzy) to detect these states despite dynamic UI layouts. Evaluated across 496 diverse tasks with four mobile agents, LlamaTouch achieves 80% accuracy in task completion detection—significantly outperforming static action-based methods—by focusing on critical UI states rather than deterministic action paths.

references.label: nong2024mobileflow
references.title: MobileFlow: A Multimodal LLM For Mobile GUI Agent

s2_metadata.title: MobileFlow: A Multimodal LLM For Mobile GUI Agent
s2_metadata.abstract: Currently, the integration of mobile Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs) is ubiquitous in most people's daily lives. And the ongoing evolution of multimodal large-scale models, such as GPT-4v, Qwen-VL-Max, has significantly bolstered the capabilities of GUI comprehension and user action analysis, showcasing the potentiality of intelligent GUI assistants. However, current GUI Agents often need to access page layout information through calling system APIs, which may pose privacy risks. Fixing GUI (such as mobile interfaces) to a certain low resolution might result in the loss of fine-grained image details. At the same time, the multimodal large models built for GUI Agents currently have poor understanding and decision-making abilities for Chinese GUI interfaces, making them difficult to apply to a large number of Chinese apps. This paper introduces MobileFlow, a multimodal large language model meticulously crafted for mobile GUI agents. Transforming from the open-source model Qwen-VL-Chat into GUI domain, MobileFlow contains approximately 21 billion parameters and is equipped with novel hybrid visual encoders, making it possible for variable resolutions of image inputs and good support for multilingual GUI. By incorporating Mixture of Experts (MoE) expansions and pioneering alignment training strategies, MobileFlow has the capacity to fully interpret image data and comprehend user instructions for GUI interaction tasks. Finally, MobileFlow outperforms Qwen-VL-Max and GPT-4v in terms of task execution by GUI agents on both public and our proposed evaluation metrics, and has been successfully deployed in real-world business contexts, proving its effectiveness for practical applications.

tldr.text: [research paper] MobileFlow introduces a multimodal large language model tailored for mobile GUI agents, featuring a hybrid visual encoder combining OpenCLIP ViT-B/32 and LayoutLMv3 pretrained on UI data to enable variable-resolution input and robust Mandarin GUI understanding. The model employs Mixture of Experts (MoE) and chain-of-thought (CoT) alignment training to improve visual-language alignment, task execution, and step-by-step reasoning, outperforming GPT-4v and Qwen-VL-Max on both public and custom GUI benchmarks, while eliminating reliance on system APIs for privacy and resolution robustness.

references.label: xu2024aguvis
references.title: Aguvis: Unified Pure Vision Agents for Autonomous GUI Interaction

s2_metadata.title: Aguvis: Unified Pure Vision Agents for Autonomous GUI Interaction
s2_metadata.abstract: Automating GUI tasks remains challenging due to reliance on textual representations, platform-specific action spaces, and limited reasoning capabilities. We introduce Aguvis, a unified vision-based framework for autonomous GUI agents that directly operates on screen images, standardizes cross-platform interactions and incorporates structured reasoning via inner monologue. To enable this, we construct Aguvis Data Collection, a large-scale dataset with multimodal grounding and reasoning annotations, and develop a two-stage training pipeline that separates GUI grounding from planning and reasoning. Experiments show that Aguvis achieves state-of-the-art performance across offline and real-world online benchmarks, marking the first fully autonomous vision-based GUI agent that operates without closed-source models. We open-source all datasets, models, and training recipes at https://aguvis-project.github.io to advance future research.

tldr.text: [research paper] AGUVIS introduces a unified vision-based framework for autonomous GUI interaction that operates directly on screen images, eliminating reliance on platform-specific textual representations like HTML or accessibility trees. It employs a two-stage training pipeline to decouple GUI grounding from planning and reasoning, incorporating structured inner monologue to enable complex, step-by-step task execution. The framework is validated on large-scale cross-platform data with multimodal grounding and reasoning annotations, achieving state-of-the-art performance in both offline and real-world settings—marking the first fully autonomous vision-based GUI agent that avoids closed-source models.

references.label: qinghong2024showui
references.title: ShowUI-$π$: Flow-based Generative Models as GUI Dexterous Hands

s2_metadata.title: ShowUI-$π$: Flow-based Generative Models as GUI Dexterous Hands
s2_metadata.abstract: Building intelligent agents capable of dexterous manipulation is essential for achieving human-like automation in both robotics and digital environments. However, existing GUI agents rely on discrete click predictions (x,y), which prohibits free-form, closed-loop trajectories (e.g. dragging a progress bar) that require continuous, on-the-fly perception and adjustment. In this work, we develop ShowUI-$\pi$, the first flow-based generative model as GUI dexterous hand, featuring the following designs: (i) Unified Discrete-Continuous Actions, integrating discrete clicks and continuous drags within a shared model, enabling flexible adaptation across diverse interaction modes; (ii) Flow-based Action Generation for drag modeling, which predicts incremental cursor adjustments from continuous visual observations via a lightweight action expert, ensuring smooth and stable trajectories; (iii) Drag Training data and Benchmark, where we manually collect and synthesize 20K drag trajectories across five domains (e.g. PowerPoint, Adobe Premiere Pro), and introduce ScreenDrag, a benchmark with comprehensive online and offline evaluation protocols for assessing GUI agents' drag capabilities. Our experiments show that proprietary GUI agents still struggle on ScreenDrag (e.g. Operator scores 13.27, and the best Gemini-2.5-CUA reaches 22.18). In contrast, ShowUI-$\pi$ achieves 26.98 with only 450M parameters, underscoring both the difficulty of the task and the effectiveness of our approach. We hope this work advances GUI agents toward human-like dexterous control in digital world. The code is available at this https URL.

tldr.text: [research paper] ShowUI-π introduces the first flow-based generative model for GUI dexterous manipulation, enabling continuous, on-the-fly trajectory generation during dragging actions (e.g., drawing, captcha solving) by unifying discrete clicks and continuous drags into a shared (x,y,m) action space. It employs flow matching to predict incremental cursor adjustments from streaming visual observations via a lightweight action expert, achieving 26.98 on the ScreenDrag benchmark—outperforming state-of-the-art proprietary agents (e.g., Operator: 13.27, Gemini-2.5-CUA: 22.18)—with only 450M parameters, demonstrating the difficulty of continuous trajectory control and the efficacy of flow-based generation.

references.label: dorka2024training
references.title: Training a Vision Language Model as Smartphone Assistant

s2_metadata.title: Training a Vision Language Model as Smartphone Assistant
s2_metadata.abstract: Addressing the challenge of a digital assistant capable of executing a wide array of user tasks, our research focuses on the realm of instruction-based mobile device control. We leverage recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) and present a visual language model (VLM) that can fulfill diverse tasks on mobile devices. Our model functions by interacting solely with the user interface (UI). It uses the visual input from the device screen and mimics human-like interactions, encompassing gestures such as tapping and swiping. This generality in the input and output space allows our agent to interact with any application on the device. Unlike previous methods, our model operates not only on a single screen image but on vision-language sentences created from sequences of past screenshots along with corresponding actions. Evaluating our method on the challenging Android in the Wild benchmark demonstrates its promising efficacy and potential.

tldr.text: [research paper] Proposes a vision-language model (VLM) agent that controls mobile devices via natural language instructions by leveraging sequences of screenshots and action transcriptions, enabling generalization across apps through UI-based interaction. Unlike prior methods relying on API calls or static screen summaries, the model uses a history of visual and textual inputs (vision-language sentences) to generate next actions via masked self-attention and next-token prediction, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the Android in the Wild benchmark with a novel VLM trained from scratch and one pretrained on vision-language tasks, where OCR inclusion enhances performance.

references.label: rafailov2024direct
references.title: Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model

s2_metadata.title: Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model
s2_metadata.abstract: While large-scale unsupervised language models (LMs) learn broad world knowledge and some reasoning skills, achieving precise control of their behavior is difficult due to the completely unsupervised nature of their training. Existing methods for gaining such steerability collect human labels of the relative quality of model generations and fine-tune the unsupervised LM to align with these preferences, often with reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). However, RLHF is a complex and often unstable procedure, first fitting a reward model that reflects the human preferences, and then fine-tuning the large unsupervised LM using reinforcement learning to maximize this estimated reward without drifting too far from the original model. In this paper we introduce a new parameterization of the reward model in RLHF that enables extraction of the corresponding optimal policy in closed form, allowing us to solve the standard RLHF problem with only a simple classification loss. The resulting algorithm, which we call Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), is stable, performant, and computationally lightweight, eliminating the need for sampling from the LM during fine-tuning or performing significant hyperparameter tuning. Our experiments show that DPO can fine-tune LMs to align with human preferences as well as or better than existing methods. Notably, fine-tuning with DPO exceeds PPO-based RLHF in ability to control sentiment of generations, and matches or improves response quality in summarization and single-turn dialogue while being substantially simpler to implement and train.

tldr.text: [research paper] Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) replaces reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) with a closed-form, binary cross-entropy objective that directly optimizes language model policies to align with human preferences. By reparameterizing the RLHF objective using a change of variables, DPO eliminates the need for reward modeling, sampling, or complex policy updates—simplifying training while achieving performance comparable to or exceeding PPO-based RLHF in sentiment control, summarization, and dialogue tasks, with superior stability and computational efficiency.

references.label: azar2024general
references.title: A General Theoretical Paradigm to Understand Learning from Human Preferences

s2_metadata.title: A General Theoretical Paradigm to Understand Learning from Human Preferences
s2_metadata.venue: International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics
s2_metadata.abstract:
The prevalent deployment of learning from human preferences through reinforcement learning (RLHF) relies on two important approximations: the first assumes that pairwise preferences can be substituted with pointwise rewards. The second assumes that a reward model trained on these pointwise rewards can generalize from collected data to out-of-distribution data sampled by the policy. Recently, Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO) has been proposed as an approach that bypasses the second approximation and learn directly a policy from collected data without the reward modelling stage. However, this method still heavily relies on the first approximation.\
  In this paper we try to gain a deeper theoretical understanding of these practical algorithms. In particular we derive a new general objective called $\\Psi$PO for learning from human preferences that is expressed in terms of pairwise preferences and therefore bypasses both approximations. This new general objective allows us to perform an in-depth analysis of the behavior of RLHF and DPO (as special cases of $\\Psi$PO) and to identify their potential pitfalls. We then consider another special case for $\\Psi$PO by setting $\\Psi$ simply to Identity, for which we can derive an efficient optimisation procedure, prove performance guarantees and demonstrate its empirical superiority to DPO on some illustrative examples.

tldr.text: [theoretical research paper] The paper introduces a general theoretical framework, \Psi\text{-PO}, for learning from human preferences that directly optimizes policies using pairwise preferences, bypassing both the pointwise reward approximation and reward modeling. It shows that standard RLHF and DPO are special cases of \Psi\text{-PO} and are vulnerable to overfitting due to reliance on Bradley-Terry models, especially when preferences are deterministic. The proposed Identity-P0 (IPO) method, which sets \Psi to identity, avoids these pitfalls by directly using pairwise preferences and is shown to outperform DPO empirically in simple bandit settings.

references.label: ethayarajh2023human
references.title: Score-oriented loss (SOL) functions

s2_metadata.title: Score-oriented loss (SOL) functions
s2_metadata.abstract: Loss functions engineering and the assessment of forecasting performances are two crucial and intertwined aspects of supervised machine learning. This paper focuses on binary classification to introduce a class of loss functions that are defined on probabilistic confusion matrices and that allow an automatic and a priori maximization of the skill scores. The performances of these loss functions are validated during the training phase of two experimental forecasting problems, thus showing that the probability distribution function associated with the confusion matrices significantly impacts the outcome of the score maximization process.

tldr.text: [research paper] The paper introduces a class of Score-Oriented Loss (SOL) functions derived from probabilistic confusion matrices, where the classification threshold is modeled as a random variable with a priori distribution. By taking the expected value of the confusion matrix under this distribution, the authors construct differentiable loss functions that automatically optimize skill scores (e.g., accuracy, F1) during training, eliminating the need for post-hoc score maximization. Theoretical analysis and numerical experiments show that the choice of threshold distribution critically influences the final score performance, with optimal thresholds aligning with the concentration regions of the prior distribution.

references.label: schulman2017proximal
references.title: Proximal Policy Optimization Smoothed Algorithm

s2_metadata.title: Proximal Policy Optimization Smoothed Algorithm
s2_metadata.abstract: Proximal policy optimization (PPO) has yielded state-of-the-art results in policy search, a subfield of reinforcement learning, with one of its key points being the use of a surrogate objective function to restrict the step size at each policy update. Although such restriction is helpful, the algorithm still suffers from performance instability and optimization inefficiency from the sudden flattening of the curve. To address this issue we present a PPO variant, named Proximal Policy Optimization Smooth Algorithm (PPOS), and its critical improvement is the use of a functional clipping method instead of a flat clipping method. We compare our method with PPO and PPORB, which adopts a rollback clipping method, and prove that our method can conduct more accurate updates at each time step than other PPO methods. Moreover, we show that it outperforms the latest PPO variants on both performance and stability in challenging continuous control tasks.

tldr.text: [research paper] PPOS introduces a functional clipping mechanism in Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to smooth the likelihood ratio updates, replacing the flat clipping with a continuous, differentiable function that better constrains policy updates without suppressing exploration. This improves both training stability and convergence speed compared to standard PPO and PPORB, especially in high-dimensional continuous control tasks, by maintaining a more accurate gradient signal while preventing abrupt policy shifts that cause instability.

references.label: luong2024reft
references.title: ReFT: Reasoning with Reinforced Fine-Tuning

s2_metadata.title: ReFT: Reasoning with Reinforced Fine-Tuning
s2_metadata.venue: Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics
s2_metadata.abstract: One way to enhance the reasoning capability of Large Language Models (LLMs) is to conduct Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) using Chain-of-Thought (CoT) annotations. This approach does not show sufficiently strong generalization ability, however, because the training only relies on the given CoT data. In math problem-solving, for example, there is usually only one annotated reasoning path for each question in the training data. Intuitively, it would be better for the algorithm to learn from multiple annotated reasoning paths given a question. To address this issue, we propose a simple yet effective approach called Reinforced Fine-Tuning (ReFT) to enhance the generalizability of learning LLMs for reasoning, with math problem-solving as an example. ReFT first warmups the model with SFT, and then employs on-line reinforcement learning, specifically the PPO algorithm in this paper, to further fine-tune the model, where an abundance of reasoning paths are automatically sampled given the question and the rewards are naturally derived from the ground-truth answers. Extensive experiments on GSM8K, MathQA, and SVAMP datasets show that ReFT significantly outperforms SFT, and the performance can be potentially further boosted by combining inference-time strategies such as majority voting and re-ranking. Note that ReFT obtains the improvement by learning from the same training questions as SFT, without relying on extra or augmented training questions. This indicates a superior generalization ability for ReFT.

tldr.text: [research paper] ReFT introduces a reinforcement learning-based fine-tuning method that enhances LLM reasoning by sampling multiple valid Chain-of-Thought paths from the same training questions during online PPO training, leveraging ground-truth answers as natural rewards. Unlike SFT, which relies on a single annotated path per question, ReFT learns from diverse reasoning trajectories, significantly improving generalization in math problem-solving without requiring additional data or reward modeling, and achieves further gains via inference-time majority voting and reranking.

references.label: zhang2024rest
references.title: ReST-MCTS*: LLM Self-Training via Process Reward Guided Tree Search

s2_metadata.title: ReST-MCTS*: LLM Self-Training via Process Reward Guided Tree Search
s2_metadata.abstract: Recent methodologies in LLM self-training mostly rely on LLM generating responses and filtering those with correct output answers as training data. This approach often yields a low-quality fine-tuning training set (e.g., incorrect plans or intermediate reasoning). In this paper, we develop a reinforced self-training approach, called ReST-MCTS*, based on integrating process reward guidance with tree search MCTS* for collecting higher-quality reasoning traces as well as per-step value to train policy and reward models. ReST-MCTS* circumvents the per-step manual annotation typically used to train process rewards by tree-search-based reinforcement learning: Given oracle final correct answers, ReST-MCTS* is able to infer the correct process rewards by estimating the probability this step can help lead to the correct answer. These inferred rewards serve dual purposes: they act as value targets for further refining the process reward model and also facilitate the selection of high-quality traces for policy model self-training. We first show that the tree-search policy in ReST-MCTS* achieves higher accuracy compared with prior LLM reasoning baselines such as Best-of-N and Tree-of-Thought, within the same search budget. We then show that by using traces searched by this tree-search policy as training data, we can continuously enhance the three language models for multiple iterations, and outperform other self-training algorithms such as ReST$^\\text{EM}$ and Self-Rewarding LM. We release all code at https://github.com/THUDM/ReST-MCTS.

tldr.text: [research paper] ReST-MCTS∗ introduces a model-based reinforcement learning framework that uses MCTS∗ with process reward guidance to generate high-quality reasoning traces without manual per-step annotations. It infers per-step rewards via tree search rollouts using oracle final answers, enabling self-training with accurate value signals that improve both policy and reward model performance. The method outperforms existing baselines (e.g., Best-of-N, ReST^EM) in trace quality and accuracy under the same search budget, achieving superior reasoning performance on SciBench and MATH benchmarks through iterative self-training.

references.label: xie2024monte
references.title: Monte Carlo Tree Search Boosts Reasoning via Iterative Preference Learning

s2_metadata.title: Monte Carlo Tree Search Boosts Reasoning via Iterative Preference Learning
s2_metadata.abstract: We introduce an approach aimed at enhancing the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) through an iterative preference learning process inspired by the successful strategy employed by AlphaZero. Our work leverages Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to iteratively collect preference data, utilizing its look-ahead ability to break down instance-level rewards into more granular step-level signals. To enhance consistency in intermediate steps, we combine outcome validation and stepwise self-evaluation, continually updating the quality assessment of newly generated data. The proposed algorithm employs Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to update the LLM policy using this newly generated step-level preference data. Theoretical analysis reveals the importance of using on-policy sampled data for successful self-improving. Extensive evaluations on various arithmetic and commonsense reasoning tasks demonstrate remarkable performance improvements over existing models. For instance, our approach outperforms the Mistral-7B Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) baseline on GSM8K, MATH, and ARC-C, with substantial increases in accuracy to $81.8\\%$ (+$5.9\\%$), $34.7\\%$ (+$5.8\\%$), and $76.4\\%$ (+$15.8\\%$), respectively. Additionally, our research delves into the training and inference compute tradeoff, providing insights into how our method effectively maximizes performance gains. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/YuxiXie/MCTS-DPO.

tldr.text: [research paper] The paper proposes an iterative preference learning framework that uses Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to generate step-level preference signals from LLM-generated responses, enabling fine-grained policy updates via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). By breaking down instance-level rewards into granular step-level feedback through MCTS rollouts, the method achieves superior reasoning performance on arithmetic and commonsense tasks—outperforming SFT baselines by up to +15.8% on ARC-C—while maintaining consistency via self-evaluation and on-policy data collection, which is theoretically shown to be essential for stable self-improvement.

references.label: bai2024digirl
references.title: DigiRL: Training In-The-Wild Device-Control Agents with Autonomous Reinforcement Learning

s2_metadata.title: DigiRL: Training In-The-Wild Device-Control Agents with Autonomous Reinforcement Learning
s2_metadata.abstract: Training corpuses for vision language models (VLMs) typically lack sufficient amounts of decision-centric data. This renders off-the-shelf VLMs sub-optimal for decision-making tasks such as in-the-wild device control through graphical user interfaces (GUIs). While training with static demonstrations has shown some promise, we show that such methods fall short for controlling real GUIs due to their failure to deal with real-world stochasticity and non-stationarity not captured in static observational data. This paper introduces a novel autonomous RL approach, called DigiRL, for training in-the-wild device control agents through fine-tuning a pre-trained VLM in two stages: offline RL to initialize the model, followed by offline-to-online RL. To do this, we build a scalable and parallelizable Android learning environment equipped with a VLM-based evaluator and develop a simple yet effective RL approach for learning in this domain. Our approach runs advantage-weighted RL with advantage estimators enhanced to account for stochasticity along with an automatic curriculum for deriving maximal learning signal. We demonstrate the effectiveness of DigiRL using the Android-in-the-Wild (AitW) dataset, where our 1.3B VLM trained with RL achieves a 49.5% absolute improvement -- from 17.7 to 67.2% success rate -- over supervised fine-tuning with static human demonstration data. These results significantly surpass not only the prior best agents, including AppAgent with GPT-4V (8.3% success rate) and the 17B CogAgent trained with AitW data (38.5%), but also the prior best autonomous RL approach based on filtered behavior cloning (57.8%), thereby establishing a new state-of-the-art for digital agents for in-the-wild device control.

tldr.text: [research paper] DigiRL introduces an autonomous reinforcement learning framework for training vision-language models (VLMs) to perform in-the-wild device control on Android interfaces, overcoming limitations of static demonstration data by leveraging offline-to-online RL. It uses a scalable Android environment with a VLM-based evaluator to enable parallel, real-time interaction, and employs advantage-weighted regression enhanced with an instruction-level curriculum and step-level value function to extract high-fidelity, low-variance learning signals from stochastic, multi-turn interactions. The resulting 1.3B VLM achieves a 49.5% absolute improvement (17.7% → 67.2% success rate) over supervised fine-tuning, surpassing prior SOTA agents including AppAgent, CogAgent, and filtered behavior cloning, by enabling self-correction, recovery from failures, and adaptation to dynamic GUIs.

references.label: wang2024distrl
references.title: Beyond Average Return in Markov Decision Processes

s2_metadata.title: Beyond Average Return in Markov Decision Processes
s2_metadata.abstract: What are the functionals of the reward that can be computed and optimized exactly in Markov Decision Processes?In the finite-horizon, undiscounted setting, Dynamic Programming (DP) can only handle these operations efficiently for certain classes of statistics. We summarize the characterization of these classes for policy evaluation, and give a new answer for the planning problem. Interestingly, we prove that only generalized means can be optimized exactly, even in the more general framework of Distributional Reinforcement Learning (DistRL).DistRL permits, however, to evaluate other functionals approximately. We provide error bounds on the resulting estimators, and discuss the potential of this approach as well as its limitations.These results contribute to advancing the theory of Markov Decision Processes by examining overall characteristics of the return, and particularly risk-conscious strategies.

tldr.text: [theoretical research paper] The paper characterizes the exact functionals of the reward that can be optimized via dynamic programming in finite-horizon, undiscounted MDPs, proving that only generalized means (e.g., median, variance-weighted means) admit exact optimization—regardless of whether using classical DP or Distributional RL (DistRL). It establishes that DistRL cannot extend the class of exactly computable functionals beyond what is already supported by standard dynamic programming, and provides rigorous error bounds on approximate evaluation of other functionals in DistRL, highlighting its limitations in risk-sensitive planning.

references.label: wu2025reachagent
references.title: ReachAgent: Enhancing Mobile Agent via Page Reaching and Operation

s2_metadata.title: ReachAgent: Enhancing Mobile Agent via Page Reaching and Operation
s2_metadata.venue: North American Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics
s2_metadata.abstract: Recently, mobile AI agents have gained increasing attention. Given a task, mobile AI agents can interact with mobile devices in multiple steps and finally form a GUI flow that solves the task. However, existing agents tend to focus on most task-relevant elements at each step, leading to local optimal solutions and ignoring the overall GUI flow. To address this issue, we constructed a training dataset called MobileReach, which breaks the task into page reaching and operation subtasks. Furthermore, we propose ReachAgent, a two-stage framework that focuses on improving its task-completion abilities. It utilizes the page reaching and page operation subtasks, along with reward-based preference GUI flows, to further enhance the agent. Experimental results show that ReachAgent significantly improves the IoU Acc and Text Acc by 7.12% and 7.69% on the step-level and 4.72% and 4.63% on the task-level compared to the SOTA agent. Our data and code will be released upon acceptance.

tldr.text: [research paper] ReachAgent introduces a two-stage framework that decomposes mobile control tasks into page reaching and page operation subtasks, using a custom dataset (MobileReach) to train agents on subtask-specific abilities. The framework incorporates a 4-level reward function to learn preference-based GUI flows and an action alignment mechanism to reduce candidate action space, resulting in improved IoU and text accuracy across both step-level and task-level benchmarks by 4.72%–7.69% over SOTA agents.

references.label: jiao2025tcpo
references.title: TCPO: Thought-Centric Preference Optimization for Effective Embodied Decision-making

s2_metadata.title: TCPO: Thought-Centric Preference Optimization for Effective Embodied Decision-making
s2_metadata.venue: Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing
s2_metadata.abstract: Using effective generalization capabilities of vision language models (VLMs) in context-specific dynamic tasks for embodied artificial intelligence remains a significant challenge. Although supervised fine-tuned models can better align with the real physical world, they still exhibit sluggish responses and hallucination issues in dynamically changing environments, necessitating further alignment. Existing post-SFT methods, reliant on reinforcement learning and chain-of-thought (CoT) approaches, are constrained by sparse rewards and action-only optimization, resulting in low sample efficiency, poor consistency, and model degradation. To address these issues, this paper proposes Thought-Centric Preference Optimization (TCPO) for effective embodied decision-making. Specifically, TCPO introduces a stepwise preference-based optimization approach, transforming sparse reward signals into richer step sample pairs. It emphasizes the alignment of the model's intermediate reasoning process, mitigating the problem of model degradation. Moreover, by incorporating Action Policy Consistency Constraint (APC), it further imposes consistency constraints on the model output. Experiments in the ALFWorld environment demonstrate an average success rate of 26.67%, achieving a 6% improvement over RL4VLM and validating the effectiveness of our approach in mitigating model degradation after fine-tuning. These results highlight the potential of integrating preference-based learning techniques with CoT processes to enhance the decision-making capabilities of vision-language models in embodied agents.

tldr.text: [research paper] TCPO introduces a thought-centric preference optimization framework that replaces action-only reinforcement learning with stepwise preference alignment over chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning, transforming sparse rewards into rich trajectory pairs. By optimizing intermediate reasoning steps instead of final actions, TCPO mitigates model degradation and credit assignment errors, while the Action Policy Consistency Constraint (APC) enforces that generated actions strictly derive from the CoT process, preserving linguistic and policy consistency. Experiments in ALFWorld show a 6% improvement in task success rate over RL4VLM baselines, demonstrating superior long-horizon decision-making and robustness in dynamic environments.

references.label: li2025treepo
references.title: TreePO: Bridging the Gap of Policy Optimization and Efficacy and Inference Efficiency with Heuristic Tree-based Modeling

s2_metadata.title: TreePO: Bridging the Gap of Policy Optimization and Efficacy and Inference Efficiency with Heuristic Tree-based Modeling

tldr.text: [research paper] TreePO introduces a tree-based rollout framework that models sequence generation as a hierarchical search process to reduce redundant computation in reinforcement learning for large language models. By leveraging shared prefixes through KV cache amortization and introducing dynamic divergence with probability-driven fallback, it achieves up to 43% reduction in GPU hours and 40–35% savings in trajectory- and token-level compute, while preserving or improving exploration diversity and enabling credit assignment at the segment level without requiring prior supervised fine-tuning.

references.label: hou2025treerl
references.title: TreeRL: LLM Reinforcement Learning with On-Policy Tree Search

s2_metadata.title: TreeRL: LLM Reinforcement Learning with On-Policy Tree Search

tldr.text: [research paper] TreeRL introduces an on-policy reinforcement learning framework for LLMs that integrates efficient tree search (EPTree) to generate diverse reasoning paths and provide fine-grained, process-level supervision. Unlike traditional i.i.d. multi-chain sampling, EPTree branches from high-entropy intermediate tokens, reducing token cost while enabling effective exploration; the resulting tree structure directly yields advantage-based process rewards that are resistant to reward hacking and eliminate the need for separate reward model training. Experiments on math and code reasoning benchmarks show TreeRL outperforms ChainRL by leveraging both improved exploration and on-policy process signals derived from the same generation trajectory.

references.label: guo2025segment
references.title: Segment Policy Optimization: Effective Segment-Level Credit Assignment in RL for Large Language Models

s2_metadata.title: Segment Policy Optimization: Effective Segment-Level Credit Assignment in RL for Large Language Models

tldr.text: [research paper] Segment Policy Optimization (SPO) introduces a mid-grained RL framework for large language models that partitions generated sequences into segments to enable more accurate and efficient credit assignment than token-level (e.g., PPO) or trajectory-level (e.g., GRPO) methods. By leveraging Monte Carlo advantage estimation at the segment level without a critic model, SPO achieves precise feedback with reduced estimation overhead, using novel cutpoint-based partitioning for short chains-of-thought (SPO-chain) and tree-based advantage estimation for long chains (SPO-tree), with 6–12% and 7–11% accuracy gains over baseline methods on GSM8K and MATH500, respectively. A probability-mask strategy selectively computes losses for key tokens, improving optimization efficiency.

topics.label: Mobile GUI Agent
topics.summary: LLMs and VLMs are increasingly used to build autonomous mobile GUI agents, both closed-source (e.g., GPT-4V) and open-source (e.g., SeeClick). Our method organizes trajectory data into multi-turn dialogues using the CoaT thinking pattern to avoid limited action capabilities.

topics.label: Reinforcement Learning
topics.summary: RL algorithms like DPO, IPO, KTO, PPO are used for human preference alignment in NLP. Works like ReFT, ReST-MCTS*, Xie et al., DigiRL, DistRL, ReachAgent, TCPO, TreePO, TreeRL, SPO apply RL for reasoning or GUI agents. Our T-DPO method with CoaT-tree offers more efficient sampling and training without unstable PRMs.

key_points.text: LLMs are increasingly used as autonomous agents for mobile interaction.
Researchers build mobile GUI agents and multi-agent frameworks based on closed-source VLMs.
Some researchers train agents with stronger element grounding, page navigation, GUI understanding, and task planning based on open-source VLMs.
Our method organizes trajectory data into multi-turns of dialogues based on the CoaT thinking pattern.
Algorithms applied in NLP for human preference alignment include DPO, IPO, KTO, and PPO.
ReFT adopts reinforcement learning as a fine-tuning paradigm to improve performance on math problems.
ReST-MCTS* focuses on higher-quality step reward, where the process reward model is important.
Xie et al. label preference via MCTS based on feedback from self-evaluation.
Digirl and Distrl use online trajectory collection for mobile GUI agents, but the process is very slow.
Reachagent uses DPO training to compare the quality of multiple actions.
TCPO optimizes thoughts but does not explicitly enforce thought–action consistency.
TreePO, TreeRL, and SPO segment long sequences into many short segments, leading to high computational cost and low data efficiency.
In contrast, our method models thoughts with a fixed CoaT-tree and uses T-DPO to optimize the thinking process, while step values are computed directly from rule-based rewards, without unstable PRMs.
Our design yields more efficient sampling and training, especially in GUI-agent settings.
Warm-up fine-tuning of a general VLM to a mobile GUI domain agent with basic capabilities is performed.

topics.label: VLM-based Mobile GUI Agents
topics.summary: Mobile GUI agents use VLMs to interact with smartphone interfaces. Key works include AITW dataset, CogAgent, AppAgent, Auto-UI, and subsequent open-source agents like SeeClick, UGround, OS-ATLAS, UI-TARS, Falcon-UI. Datasets and benchmarks such as AMEX and AndroidControl support training and evaluation.

topics.label: Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in GUI Tasks
topics.summary: Reasoning is critical for multi-step GUI tasks. Android in the Zoo proposes CoaT paradigm. CoT effectiveness is nuanced; inconsistent gains observed empirically. UI-TARS integrates System-2 reasoning.

topics.label: Self-Training and Preference Optimization for Reasoning
topics.summary: Self-training and DPO improve reasoning. Step-DPO and DeepSeek-R1 show progress. In GUI domain, GUI-G1 analyzes R1-Zero-like training challenges.

topics.label: Monte Carlo Tree Search for LLM Reasoning
topics.summary: MCTS is adapted for LLM reasoning. Tree of Thoughts, Xie et al.'s MCTS-DPO, ReST-MCTS*, and CPO use tree search to generate preference signals or reasoning traces. These methods often require PRMs, costly for GUI.

topics.label: MobileIPL (Our Work)
topics.summary: MobileIPL constructs a CoaT-tree with rule-based rewards and backward credit assignment, avoiding PRM. It operates in visually grounded mobile GUI domain and evaluates both final actions and intermediate reasoning steps via T-DPO.

claims.text: Mobile GUI agents autonomously interact with smartphone interfaces, driven by advances in VLMs.
The Android in the Wild (AITW) dataset provides a large-scale benchmark for device-control systems.
CogAgent achieves strong visual understanding of GUI screenshots and state-of-the-art results on multiple GUI benchmarks.
AppAgent uses multimodal LLMs to operate smartphone applications through exploration.
Auto-UI introduces a chain-of-action mechanism for screen-level interaction.
Recent work focuses on open-source VLM-based GUI agents approaching commercial model capabilities.

UGround introduces a pixel-level visual grounding framework achieving strong results without text.
OS-ATLAS develops a foundation action model for cross-platform GUI grounding with strong out-of-distribution generalization.
UI-TARS pioneers a native GUI agent with System-2 reasoning and iterative online training.
Falcon-UI proposes a decoupled training paradigm where GUI context understanding precedes instruction-following.
Several datasets and benchmarks facilitate mobile GUI agent research.
AMEX introduces multi-level annotations for robust agent training.
AndroidControl provides a large-scale dataset for studying generalization in UI control agents.
Reasoning is critical for GUI agents requiring multi-step planning and context-aware decisions.
Android in the Zoo introduces the CoaT paradigm for semantic reasoning in GUI navigation.
CoaT improves performance on complex GUI actions when combined with AITZ fine-tuning.
The thinking-decision-grounding triplet of CoaT is an effective reasoning structure in GUI works.
The effectiveness of CoT reasoning for GUI agents is nuanced.
CoT reasoning yields inconsistent performance gains in mobile GUI tasks, with improvements in interactive but not static benchmarks.
Reasoning benefits depend on model architecture, task characteristics, and benchmark design.
UI-TARS incorporates System-2 reasoning with task decomposition and milestone recognition.
Self-training improves language model reasoning capabilities.
DPO aligns language models with preferences without a separate reward model using pairwise comparison.
Several works explore finer-grained preference optimization building on DPO.
Step-DPO treats reasoning steps as units for preference learning, improving mathematical reasoning.
DeepSeek-R1 uses large-scale RL with GRPO to incentivize reasoning without supervised fine-tuning.
Self-training approaches are adapted for GUI agent reasoning.
GUI-G1 identifies challenges in R1-Zero-like training for GUI grounding: image token overload, reward hacking, length biases.
GUI-G1's Fast Thinking Template and box-size-constrained reward achieve strong results with limited data.
MCTS is adapted from game-playing to enhance LLM reasoning.
Tree of Thoughts uses tree-structured search for LLM planning, improving tasks requiring lookahead.
Xie et al. propose MCTS-based iterative preference learning for step-level policy updates via DPO.
Granular step-level feedback yields superior reasoning; on-policy data collection is essential.
ReST-MCTS* uses MCTS with process reward guidance to generate reasoning traces without manual annotations.
ReST-MCTS* infers per-step rewards via tree search rollouts for self-training.
GUI domain requires costly step-level reward annotation with real devices, making PRM-based methods challenging.
CPO constructs pairwise preference data from tree-of-thought search trees without external rewards or human annotations.
CPO aligns LLMs with optimal reasoning trajectories using preferred/pruned steps, achieving low-latency performance comparable to tree search.
MobileIPL differs from prior approaches in several key aspects.
MobileIPL constructs a CoaT-tree with rule-based rewards and backward credit assignment, avoiding PRM.
MobileIPL operates in the visually grounded mobile GUI domain with multi-turn dialogues conditioned on UI screenshots, unlike text-based approaches.
MobileIPL evaluates both final action and intermediate reasoning steps using Thinking-level DPO.

topic_alignment.rationale: Gold topic 'Mobile GUI Agent' aligns primarily with candidate STopic1 on VLM-based mobile GUI agents, covering key datasets and models. STopic2 on CoT reasoning in GUI tasks also relates closely as a subarea of GUI agent reasoning, so a soft alignment to both is appropriate.

Gold topic 'Reinforcement Learning' covers RL algorithms for preference alignment, which maps to STopic3 (self-training/DPO), STopic4 (MCTS for reasoning), and STopic5 (MobileIPL with T-DPO). The candidate topics collectively address the RL methods mentioned, with STopic5 directly presenting the proposed method. High coverage and relevance.

topic_granularity.rationale: The candidate splits the two gold topics into five more specific sub-topics (GUI agents, CoT reasoning, self-training, MCTS, and the proposed method). This granularity is appropriate as it separates key themes without being overly fragmented. Slightly lower score because some sub-topics (e.g., CoT) could be subsumed under a broader heading, but overall it provides good detail.

topic_ordering.rationale: The ordering flows logically: basic mobile GUI agents (STopic1), then reasoning within GUI (STopic2), then optimization methods (STopic3, STopic4), and finally the proposed method (STopic5). This progression from general to specific and from background to contribution is clear and effective. Minor deduction because STopic2 on CoT could have been placed immediately after STopic1 for tighter coupling.

synthesis_quality.rationale: The section effectively builds a landscape of mobile GUI agents, covering key closed- and open-source models, datasets, and benchmarks. It provides method comparisons (e.g., grounding pre-training vs. pixel-level grounding) and highlights limitations (e.g., cost of process reward models in GUI). The transition from existing work to the proposed MobileIPL method clearly motivates the gap—avoiding manual step annotations and operating in visual domains. However, the synthesis omits several important works from the gold-standard set (e.g., DigiRL, Distrl, TCPO, TreePO, TreeRL, SPO, IPO, PPO, ReFT), which weakens comprehensiveness. The coverage of preference optimization and MCTS is strong but could be more inclusive of closely related GUI-agent RL methods.

writing_quality.rationale: The writing maintains a consistent academic tone and uses precise terminology (e.g., 'CoaT paradigm', 'grounding pre-training', 'T-DPO'). Sentences are mostly clear and concise, with logical flow between subsections. The structure helps readability. Minor verbosity appears in a few places (e.g., the first sentence), but overall the style is efficient and well-organized. Terminology is consistent throughout.
issues: Occasional long sentences could be split for better clarity (e.g., the first sentence of the section).
Some redundancy in the MCTS section: 'extensively adapted' and 'deliberate problem-solving framework' could be tightened.

topic_alignment.rationale: Gold topic 'Mobile GUI Agent' aligns primarily with candidate STopic1 on VLM-based mobile GUI agents, covering key datasets and models. STopic2 on CoT reasoning in GUI tasks also relates closely as a subarea of GUI agent reasoning, so a soft alignment to both is appropriate.

Gold topic 'Reinforcement Learning' covers RL algorithms for preference alignment, which maps to STopic3 (self-training/DPO), STopic4 (MCTS for reasoning), and STopic5 (MobileIPL with T-DPO). The candidate topics collectively address the RL methods mentioned, with STopic5 directly presenting the proposed method. High coverage and relevance.

granularity.rationale: The candidate splits the two gold topics into five more specific sub-topics (GUI agents, CoT reasoning, self-training, MCTS, and the proposed method). This granularity is appropriate as it separates key themes without being overly fragmented. Slightly lower score because some sub-topics (e.g., CoT) could be subsumed under a broader heading, but overall it provides good detail.

ordering.rationale: The ordering flows logically: basic mobile GUI agents (STopic1), then reasoning within GUI (STopic2), then optimization methods (STopic3, STopic4), and finally the proposed method (STopic5). This progression from general to specific and from background to contribution is clear and effective. Minor deduction because STopic2 on CoT could have been placed immediately after STopic1 for tighter coupling.

details.rationale: The section effectively builds a landscape of mobile GUI agents, covering key closed- and open-source models, datasets, and benchmarks. It provides method comparisons (e.g., grounding pre-training vs. pixel-level grounding) and highlights limitations (e.g., cost of process reward models in GUI). The transition from existing work to the proposed MobileIPL method clearly motivates the gap—avoiding manual step annotations and operating in visual domains. However, the synthesis omits several important works from the gold-standard set (e.g., DigiRL, Distrl, TCPO, TreePO, TreeRL, SPO, IPO, PPO, ReFT), which weakens comprehensiveness. The coverage of preference optimization and MCTS is strong but could be more inclusive of closely related GUI-agent RL methods.
issues: Missing coverage of key RL-based GUI agent methods: DigiRL, Distrl, ReachAgent, TCPO, TreePO, TreeRL, SPO.
Missing general alignment algorithms: IPO, PPO, ReFT (only DPO, Step-DPO, DeepSeek-R1 covered).
The candidate's own method section is placed as a separate subsection rather than integrated into comparisons, slightly reducing synthesis flow.

details.rationale: The writing maintains a consistent academic tone and uses precise terminology (e.g., 'CoaT paradigm', 'grounding pre-training', 'T-DPO'). Sentences are mostly clear and concise, with logical flow between subsections. The structure helps readability. Minor verbosity appears in a few places (e.g., the first sentence), but overall the style is efficient and well-organized. Terminology is consistent throughout.
issues: Occasional long sentences could be split for better clarity (e.g., the first sentence of the section).
Some redundancy in the MCTS section: 'extensively adapted' and 'deliberate problem-solving framework' could be tightened.
